title: July | 2026 | FRED Blog
gist: This is a FRED Blog monthly index page (July 2026): most of the text is navigation, an archive list, and a set of recent-post titles. It fully reproduces exactly one analytical post — 'Durable goods inflation and effective tariffs' — which documents how the U.S. effective tariff rate more than quadrupled to over 11% in 2025 and coincided with durable goods prices flipping from ~3% year-over-year deflation to ~2-3% inflation.

insights.claim: From mid-2023 through early 2025, U.S. durable goods prices (appliances, electronics, furniture) were in deflation, declining by as much as 3% year-over-year.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: The prior deflationary baseline for durables is the reference point investors should watch to gauge how much of recent goods inflation is tariff-driven versus structural.
insights.quote: Deflation was the norm from mid-2023 through early 2025, with prices declining by as much as 3% year-over-year.
insights.locator: Dramatic shifts in durable goods prices and trade policy

insights.claim: By early 2026, U.S. durable goods prices had reversed from deflation to increases of around 2% to 3% year-over-year.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: A swing of roughly 5 percentage points in the durables inflation contribution is material for headline PCE and for Fed policy expectations.
insights.quote: durable goods deflation reversed course, from declines to increases of around 2% to 3% by early 2026.

insights.claim: The U.S. effective tariff rate stayed relatively stable at around 2.5% through 2024, then surged to over 11% during 2025, more than quadrupling in about a year.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: A more-than-4x jump in the effective tariff burden is a first-order shock to import-dependent supply chains and consumer goods margins.
insights.quote: The effective tariff rate surged from roughly 2.5% to over 11%, more than quadrupling in about a year.

insights.claim: The effective tariff rate is defined as total tariff revenue collected by the government divided by the total value of all imported goods.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Knowing the metric is revenue-over-import-value lets investors reconstruct it from customs receipts and import data rather than relying on headline statutory rates.
insights.quote: the effective tariff rate is total tariff revenue collected by the government divided by the total value of all imported goods.
insights.locator: Dramatic shifts in durable goods prices and trade policy

insights.claim: Effective tariffs appear to have peaked in late 2025 and have since begun declining, which if sustained could moderate durable goods inflation and potentially return prices to their earlier declines.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: A rolling-over tariff rate signals possible disinflation in goods, a tailwind for the Fed and for rate-sensitive positioning if it holds.
insights.quote: effective tariffs appear to have peaked in late 2025 and have since begun declining. If this trend continues, we could see a corresponding moderation in durable goods inflation

insights.claim: The 2025 tariff spike and the acceleration in durable goods prices coincided closely, suggesting import taxes may be a factor ending the earlier durable goods deflation, though prices reflect many other factors.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: If tariffs are a genuine driver of goods reflation, tariff policy becomes a leading indicator for the goods component of inflation and for consumer-facing pricing power.
insights.quote: the coinciding tariff spike and price acceleration suggest import taxes may be playing a role in ending the deflation consumers experienced with durable goods

themes.theme: Index page — surfaced but not-reproduced posts

insights.claim: The July 2026 FRED Blog page lists additional recent St. Louis Fed research posts by title only — on AI investment and semiconductor prices, U.S. household financial optimism (Bankrate Consumer Survey), the June 2026 FOMC Summary of Economic Projections, and household economic well-being (SHED) — without reproducing their content.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Flags adjacent FRED research topics worth pulling directly, but this record carries only their headlines, not extractable data.
